title: Add `ContainerManager` annotation to created containers
body:
Hey :wave:, I had this idea to improve the distinction between container
runtimes to avoid issues with removed containers like in https://github.com/cri-o/cri-o/pull/2761.

This change adds the following annotation to every container created by
podman:

```json
"Annotations": {
    "io.containers.manager": "libpod"
}
```

Target of this annotaions is to indicate which project in the containers
ecosystem is the major manager of a container when applications share
the same storage paths. This way projects can decide if they want to
manipulate the container or not. For example, since CRI-O and podman are
not using the same container library (libpod), CRI-O can skip podman
containers and provide the end user more useful information.

~~Related projects like buildah and CRI-O could use the pre-defined
`ContainerManagerCRIO` and `ContainerManagerBuildah` types, whereas
podman would now be able to tell the end user which tool created the
container as well.~~

A corresponding end-to-end test has been adapted as well.

Relates to: https://github.com/cri-o/cri-o/pull/2761

comments.body:
@mheon @baude @vrothberg @TomSweeneyRedHat PTAL
@mheon also added a namespaces field to libpod a while back, that maybe could be used to help with this.
I like the idea of opt-in, and defaulting to libpod, We can obviously get buildah and podman to follow the rules so that error messages from the other tools would give more information.

> > Related projects like buildah and CRI-O could use the pre-defined
> > ContainerManagerCRIO and ContainerManagerBuildah types, whereas
> > podman would now be able to tell the end user which tool created the
> > container as well.
> 
> I don't think we should store them here. We don't allow buildah to vendor libpod (to avoid cycles on the source-package level); each project can set it independently.

Alright, I removed them for now.

> I am unable to decide whether I like the proposed change or not. Ultimately, I believe that the tools should _not_ share the container storage if that's a problem - especially in production. 

I thought that it would be intended to use podman as debugging addition to crictl for CRI-O, especially if they share libpod in the future, or am I wrong?

> I thought that it would be intended to use podman as debugging addition to crictl for CRI-O, especially if they share libpod in the future, or am I wrong?

In this case, Podman can be pointed to CRI-O's storage or be pointed to it's namespace. But for production use-cases I strongly suggest to keep them separate.

I guess I am a bit scared of that change as it can open the door for odd scenarios/use cases:
* Do we need a `podman ps --all-managers`?
* Do we extend the same idea to images?

One thing to consider: it might break current users who rely (for whatever reason) on sharing the containers.

@vrothberg we already have the issue with buildah and podman sharing the same storage for "different" containers.  Podman rm --all --force, I believe currently will remove buildah containers that it does not know about.  @TomSweeneyRedHat has a issue to work on making podman better understand `buildah` containers.
I think your test changes are in the wrong test - you grabbed an image test, not a container test. I don't actually see a test for plain `podman inspect` on a container; you might need to add one.

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *mheon*, *saschagrunert* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [mheon] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

I don't want to rely on annotations like this within Podman or Libpod; we have better ways of knowing that we launched a container, and I don't want to special-case functionality within Libpod based on it. However, I see definite value in providing this for external programs.

My preference would actually be to have a generic `libpod` that can be overridden by whoever is making the spec that is fed in; so Podman could override with `podman`, CRI-O migrated to libpod would set `cri-o`, etc.
I changed it as suggested and added a separate end to end test.
prow is currently stuck, @baude will take care of it
